locked_facts: Van de Graaff generator accumulates charge on a hollow metal dome to produce very high voltages
Charge is sprayed onto a moving belt at the bottom and transferred to the dome at the top
Charge moves to the outer surface of the dome (conductor property) — allowing more charge to be added inside
Maximum voltage limited by breakdown of surrounding air (~3×10⁶ V/m for air)
V = kQ/R — larger dome radius allows higher voltage before breakdown
Used to accelerate charged particles for nuclear physics experiments
The principle relies on the fact that charge resides on the outer surface of a conductor
truth_statements: The Van de Graaff generator works on the principle that charge placed inside a hollow conductor moves to its outer surface
The belt continuously carries charge from the spray comb at the bottom to the collecting comb at the top
The maximum achievable voltage depends on the dome radius and the breakdown field of the surrounding medium
Larger domes achieve higher voltages because V = kQ/R and breakdown occurs at E = kQ/R² = V/R
The generator can produce potentials of several million volts

aha_moment.what_to_show: Cross-section of the dome showing charge being deposited inside by the belt, then immediately migrating to the outer surface — this makes room for MORE charge to be added. The self-repulsion of charges drives them outward, creating an unlimited accumulation cycle.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Animated charge migration from inner belt contact to outer surface, with charge density building up frame by frame
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students don't understand why charge keeps accumulating — seeing the inner-to-outer migration shows why the dome never 'fills up'

simulation_states.label: Generator structure
simulation_states.description: Cross-section showing hollow metal dome, insulating belt, motor, spray comb (bottom), collecting comb (top). Labels on each part.

simulation_states.label: Charging process
simulation_states.description: Belt moves upward carrying positive charges. Spray comb ionises air at bottom. Collecting comb transfers charge to dome interior. Charge immediately moves to outer surface.
simulation_states.emphasis: Charge sprayed onto belt → carried up → transferred to dome → migrates to outer surface

simulation_states.label: Accumulation cycle
simulation_states.description: Multiple cycles shown. Dome charge builds up. Voltage meter rises. Interior always has zero net charge (all on surface). Eventually sparking occurs when E exceeds air breakdown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Interior stays empty → more charge can always be added → voltage keeps rising

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student controls belt speed and dome radius. Voltage and surface charge density update. At breakdown field, sparks appear. Larger dome → higher max voltage before breakdown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Increase dome radius — reach higher voltage before spark. Faster belt — charge accumulates faster.

common_misconceptions.belief: The dome gets 'full' and can't accept more charge
common_misconceptions.correction: Charge on the interior immediately moves to the outer surface, leaving the interior neutral. This means new charge can always be deposited inside — there is no 'full' state. The limit is air breakdown, not dome capacity.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show interior emptying after each charge deposit — dome never 'fills up'

common_misconceptions.correction: The belt only transports charge. The spray comb at the bottom ionises air using a high voltage source, depositing charge onto the belt. The belt is just a mechanical conveyor.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Highlight the spray comb as the charge source, belt as transporter
